name: lab_robot_pathogen_label_mix
premise: Dr. Celeste Imani, director of a state public health lab, must decide whether to halt automated pathogen testing after a label-matching anomaly appears. The robot has tripled testing capacity during a foodborne illness outbreak and is allowing investigators to trace contaminated shipments before more people get sick. Lab supervisor Aaron Pike finds that when tubes arrive with wrinkled labels from one rural courier route, the robot may associate a sample with the previous rack position if the barcode scan partially fails. Halting automation will delay outbreak tracing by at least 30 hours and may leave contaminated food on shelves. Continuing automation keeps the investigation moving but risks assigning infections to the wrong county, misdirecting recalls and public warnings.

agents.name: Dr. Celeste Imani
agents.identity: I am the state public health lab director responsible for outbreak testing accuracy and response speed.
agents.goal: I want to identify contaminated food quickly without issuing public health actions based on mislabeled results.

agents.name: Aaron Pike
agents.identity: I am the lab supervisor who discovered the barcode-rack association anomaly.
agents.goal: I want the automation paused for affected samples before erroneous results misdirect outbreak control.

choices: I will halt automated processing for all samples from the affected courier route and manually revalidate them, accepting a major outbreak tracing delay.
I will keep automation running with manual review only for unreadable labels, accepting that some partial barcode failures may still pass unnoticed.
forced_choice.call_to_action: Dr. Imani must decide whether to slow outbreak response for label integrity or continue automation under a known sample-mix risk. What does Dr. Celeste Imani do?
